sentences.text: Very mixed.
We had an artist, we had a doctor, and we had people who had business.
It was a very mixed group, but they were all friends.
Well, as friends, we asked them.
We didn't start the group, so I don't remember exactly.
Yeah, I think Jan Bartelsman started the group.
And so he asked us to become-- to help.
He didn't say, do you want to be a member?
Because you were not a member, because you just worked with the group.
Well, we talked to the Jews about the group, of course.
Yeah.
And the ones who were hidden with us, for instance, knew all about the group.
Yes, some.
Those two famous musicians, I [INAUDIBLE] they were friends before the war already, yeah.
Beg your pardon?
Not very large.
We had entry-- three bedrooms and a big dining room and a living room.
And in the garden, we had a studio which had two little bedrooms with it.
Because when I was divorced and my husband left me, I rented a house to have an income, and I lived in the studio, and in those two little bedrooms that were there.
They were actually not even rooms.
They were just--
My old family lived in a very good house, very big house.
No.
No, no, no.
My house, I had rented it.
When we were still married?
In the main house.
In a very nice big house.
Yeah.
I have to think when I--
Well, in the main house, in Leiden.
Somewhere in that-- later we went in ourselves.
No, no, no.
That's not true.
Before that, we went in ourselves.
I had rented it with them, and I got too many people to hide.
The people who rented it went out, and I lived here myself and could have-- my husband and I could have many more people hidden.
Well, the living room and the dining room were available for anybody.
They were there all day.
And we had our own bedroom.
When we became more crowded, we took the children in our bedroom with us.
And there was a big attic, and that you-- there was a ladder in the attic which you could let down.
Oh yeah, indeed.
And that was always down, yeah, that ladder.
Except when the Jews went in it, and it could be a lot of Jews in that attic.
It was a very large attic.
And then they put the ladder up and closed it, so you could hardly see that there wasn't a place there.
Yeah.
There was a window in it.
Yeah?
I don't know.
Well, sometimes, yeah, they came all down.
We didn't keep them in lock.
No, not on regular days.
But as soon as there was a Nazi raid, then of course they have to go and disappear.
But usually then they left the house through the tunnel and escaped that way.
Because we almost always knew when there was a-- that we had a raid totally unexpectedly, that has happened, oh, maybe one or two times.
But we always got this two-ring telephone.
Everybody would go immediately.
No, little.
Some clothes.
Yeah.
It would have been obvious, yeah.
Well, they gave us a suitcase, and they looked very Jewish.
Then it was obvious.
Oh, yeah.
But that never happened.
We always got a warning on the telephone.
No.
It was rather-- it was a dead end road with a few villas.
And so they were not too close.
We had a garden surrounding all our house.
A garden.
No.
Yeah.
Well, we never talked about it.
They were just friends living with us.
And we never discussed-- our oldest son, he knew all about it.
Yeah.
No.
But he knew.
And he sometimes helped us even.
One time, there was a-- where was it again where they had to bring something away?
I don't remember exactly who it was.
But anyway, he knew about it.
And we-- oh, yeah, we had false red papers.
And when we saw the Germans coming, when we saw that car, they always came in-- any car was always German, because we were not allowed to have cars anymore.
And we saw that car stopping front of town.
Then they had to come all the way there yet.
And so I had-- we had just been sitting at the table with the Jewish people, with their passports, or their identification papers, which I had.
And because there was so much danger in the village the previous days, they had decided to go away for a few days to their alternate addresses.
And so I sat at the table with those peoples, and then I saw the car stopping.
And so the Jewish people went at the back of the house and went away from there.
But I was sitting there with all those papers.
So I took them, and I put them in my son's shirt.
And I said to him, go away, very greatly.
He knew all about it.
He was nine years old only.
And he took a ball, and he went out of the front door and said very politely, how do you do?
To the people who came, those damn Germans.
And he took a ball, and he against the wall.
He ball-- with his ball.
And he went a little further, and a little further.
Than I saw him disappear.
And oh, was I thankful that he made it!
Which son was this?
That was Peter, our oldest.
Well, they had addresses.
Yeah, we had addresses.
Walked.
Yeah.
No, nothing was safe in the war.
Well, some went with bicycles and some walked.
Well, I did the work with all the other women together.
The Jewish women, yeah.
And well, we had 32 Jewish people hidden during the war, but not all at the same time.
The most we had at the same time was 14, but that was only a very short period.
And so we did the work together, the housework and household and all.
Very different sources.
We got coupons.
And Because I was German, and my two daughters were German, I got double the coupons.
So that instead of three, I got six.
And that helped already, a lot.
And then we got some coupons from the organization, from the underground organization.
And so we divided everything.
We all were very starved during the war, because it was-- the last winter.
That's still known in Holland as 'the hunger winter.' And the hunger winter was terrible.
We all were-- and it was terrible because people who really were going to starve from hunger first got very thin, and then they blew up.
And that was the last stage.
So when we saw somebody blowing up, we all sacrificed a little.
Because, you know.
There was no typical meal.
Well, there was soup kitchens, and we all got coupons.
Of course, the Jewish people did not get coupons, but I got double coupons, for my two daughters and me.
And so we had to go every day to a certain place where there was usually kind of a soup.
And when you had the potato from the soup, you celebrated.
Because it was practically water.
And then there was always one or two potatoes in, still unpeeled and dirt on them.
Because it was not exactly a luxury soup.
But when you had the potato, oh boy, there was a feast.
Appointed people.
Appointed people.
It was-- it was done, I think, by the Germans.
No, no, no.
No open stores.
Yeah, no, and we had-- yeah.
I know [INAUDIBLE] was a store that was always open, yeah.
Sometimes a little margarine.
Sometimes half a bottle of milk.
Sometimes some bread.
Different-- what they got, they sold.
Yeah, if we had something to cook, we cooked it.
And we want-- we went to pick mushrooms and every kind of thing which was possibly edible from the fields, of course.
But you learn to live with less and less food.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
We shared everything.
You mean--
Oh, sometimes there were terrible quarrels.
Well, I don't know.
About anything that you quarrel about, women.
Yeah, it was mainly the women who quarreled.
You know, everybody was tense.
For five years, we were just all the way like this.
No, not the entire five years, but certainly for four years, you were tense and afraid, actually, the whole day.
You tried not to think about it all the time, but you know.
And then there were also-- I was not so worried of the Germans, because I was-- well, I was young and strong, and I was a naughty girl.
Had been always, in my life.
So I had this-- I'll get those damn Germans, you know?
I'm answer them.
But I was incredibly afraid of the airplanes that flew over our house.
Because they were either English planes with bombs to go to Germany, or German with bombs going to England.
And that route was just over our place.
And I was scared stiff of that.
And still, when I'm now at home and in the night, and airplane passes, I wake up a movement, like this.
I never get rid of that anymore.
Oh, sure.
There were quarrels.
You know, everybody was tense.
And that was one of the great things, that my husband had a way of always calming people down.
Oh sure, yeah.
Very much so.
When two women would quarrel, he would go there and sit with him and talk to them.
He was fantastic in that field.
And it was very necessary.
No.
Yeah, we got a baby.
From this woman who was the wife of the musician, of Nap De Klyn.
No-- yeah, that was the second child, yeah.
It was a girl.
Oh, no.
Oh, no.
There was no chance to go to the hospital.
A doctor friend came and helped her.
Well, I don't know if he was a member of the group, but I trusted him fully.
Then he was caught, later.
And then there came a new doctor.
And nobody knew whether we could trust him or not.
He acted very trustworthy, but you never knew, you know?
Well, it was more difficult when somebody died.
You know, we had-- not in our house, but a house of friends, there was a very nice Jewish man in there.
He was a professor, and he was a very, very great person.
And we all loved him, and he died.
One morning, they found him dead in the bed.
He had a heart attack.
And we didn't know what to do.
But he lived in Amsterdam, and he lived very near a park.
So they waited till it was dark, and they dressed him up in a coat.
And two men walked with him to that park with him dead in between them.
You know, they held him up.
And they put him on a bench.
What do you call that, you know--
A bench.
And they even put some little flowers they found near there.
And they put that in his hands, and they left him there.
And next morning, he was gone.
So the Germans probably found him.
Not in our house.
And emergency care?
There was no emergency.
You couldn't bring them to a hospital.
Because you couldn't trust the people in the hospital.
A hospital would take a Jew, but it was at his own risk.
Well, the people we knew, they were all married.
They're all young married people.
We also had single people, but I don't think there were many love stories.
You had all those things you had and loved at that time.
Our own children?
Yeah.
And I had Moana.
Yeah.
Moana was the daughter of friends of mine, where we went before it became terribly dangerous for the Jewish people.
They lived in Amsterdam.
And we told them, it's going to be more dangerous, and come to us.
And they didn't want to.
He had his principle.
He said, this comes over our people, and we have to accept it.
Which was, we said, bullshit.
You know?
We were so mad that they didn't want our help.

But in the very last moment, when the Nazi has started the raid on that street, where they lived and many other Jewish people lived, there was a German-- a Dutch electrician who always was-- he had to work for the Germans, that was not his choice-- who went ahead of them.
When they were in this house, he was already in one or two houses further, to switch off all the electricity of the houses were the Jews were taken, because they were always afraid of fire.
And he came to our house, and there lived this friend of ours with his wife and his little daughter.
And he took a chance.
He also took a chance on our life, but we didn't know that, of course.
And he said, could you bring our daughter to Leiden, to those family that she will not be caught?
Because they couldn't escape any more.
And he took it.
And that was a terrible risk he took, only your own life.
Because they didn't know whether the man was pro-German or not, pro-Hitler.
But thank God, he was good.
And he took the child in the back of his bicycle, and he bicycled, which was about 27, 28 kilometers, to Leiden and put-- rang the bell, put the girl there.
And I knew the girl, of course.
And then he said, here she is.
Her parents are taken.
And he ran away, out on the bicycle.
And we never know who he was.
We've never been able to find out who did that, which was very heroic, what he did.
Moana.
Hillfman.
And she stayed with us forever, almost.
She's still my daughter.
Because when she lived in that cooped up where they brought all the Jewish people, she lived with 21 members of her family, and not one survived.
So when the war was over, then we kept her.
And we wanted to adopt her, but the Jewish people didn't allow it.
They said, she has to grow up in a Jewish surrounding.
Well, there wasn't a Jewish-- how do you call that-- orphanage, very near us, and that's where she was placed.
After the war.
Yeah.
In the war she was with us, the whole war.
I don't understand you.
Our own children?
Our own children?
They never cried.
But the older ones, they knew where to go.
They knew it exactly.
When we said, go, then they knew it's coming.
And once, one of the Jewish children stayed, had not gone away, and she was with our children, and the Germans didn't pay any attention to her.
Although she was dark, and my two children were highly blonde.
As blonde as you can be.
But it worked.
Oh, yes.
Several times.
Because they had a feeling.
They always-- they came to our house because they knew there is something going on there.
But they didn't know-- but we also had an artist, a Jewish artist, who falsified papers.
He could beautifully do that.
Beautiful work he did.
And so that office we had there too.
Kurt was his name, Kurt Delmonte.
Kurt, K-U-R-T, Delmonte.
Yes.
He was an artist.
I have several of his works yet.
Yeah.
Well, they came and knew something is going on in this house, because they came time and time again.
But I never broke down.
I kept saying, nonsense.
I wouldn't do a thing like that.
You know?
And I am German, so I wouldn't do it-- again, 'my own people.' You know?
I faked all that, of course.
But I was German.
And then they asked me, where is your husband?
And I didn't say that I had a second husband.
I said, my husband is away in Russia, fighting.
You know?
No.
And I kept my name.
Not officially, but I introduced myself to them.
My first husband's name was Molenaar, which was a Dutch name.
His forefathers were Dutch.
Yeah, it was in my own house.
No.
No, they never.
They threatened very often to take me out, but they never did.
German officers, one or two.
And they started throwing questions, throwing questions, throwing questions till I was so tired that they hoped that I would make a mistake and slip, which I didn't.
My fear was that they would come in a moment that they all were around.
That we had no warning.
But we always had a warning.
Were I ever--
No.
No.
My husband was.
My husband, he once took my husband, and he was for three days in jail.
Because they suspected him.
Oh, yeah.
Sure they know.
Yeah, sure.
They took him from my house.
Yeah.
Oh sure, they knew, yeah.
We married in the war.
The tunnel was in the atelier, in the back of the house where most of them lived, because it had two bedrooms with it and a living room.
They had built it all out.
Because when I was divorced from my first husband, I lived here.
So we added some to it.
And the tunnel went from that place.
The tunnel was not from the house.
It was from that place.
So the Jewish people who were in the house would run from the back door immediately into there, in there.
And then there was a-- there was an opening with a wall which you could put aside.
You couldn't see that at all.
We had to try that out with all kinds of lights, and even with a glass pot and the sun shining in it.
So we knew they could never find that opening if they didn't know.
And there, they could slide that away, and he could get in the coal bin.
And from the coal bin he could get in the [INAUDIBLE].
It was not camouflaged.
Oh, outside?
Oh, yeah.
I have--
There was a coal bin.
And we made a double spot in that coal bin so that up were some coals.
And under that, it was empty.
And from there, they went in that tunnel.
Yeah.
Besides, I always let them in and closed it off myself.
And one day, we had-- we had a non-Jewish hider who was sold by the Germans.
He was the director of the Royal Blast Furnaces in Holland.
Director.
And he was not Jewish, but he had to hide because he had-- they had in the blast furnaces some very important stuff.
I don't know what the name was, [INAUDIBLE] or something, which the Germans wanted.
And he had hidden it himself, personally, the director, and hidden a letter to the arts commandant from the German commander in Holland.
And had told him, I, Director-- Van Delden was his name-- have hidden that stuff myself.
You'll never get it.
If you get me, you'll get it.
And then we hid him.
Van and then capital D-E-L-D-E-N.
He was director of Royal Blast Furnace.
Yeah.
And his son lives with us here in Woodstock.
Yeah.
No, they crawled.
Yeah.
They could sit.
Yes.
No.
No.
But there were lines, flashlights.
Nothing.
No.
You know, it was good that there was no radio, because they would have heard it.
It was not that deep.
And one day, this director from the blast furnaces-- he was such a strange guy.
He was a very nice man.
And he was not Jewish, but they sought him.
And so when he got-- went in that tunnel, he stayed in the entrance instead of going deeper in.
And, of course, I didn't know that.
And then the Germans came, and I always was standing near that entrance.
I prefer to stand on it, so that I somehow, you know?
And then I heard-- certainly heard [WHISTLING].
He was whistling.
Oh, God.
And the Germans were standing there with me.
So I did-- I suddenly stood up where I was standing-- You can't go away!
You can't go away.
Oh, I can't go away.
I did so-- [CLAPS] And I said, I'm so cold!
I'm so cold! here!
And it was not cold at all.
But he fell for it.
And he heard me trampling, and so he stopped whistling.
He suddenly realized what he was doing.
But that was one of the narrow escapes.
